reasoning: Every step of the claimed chain is verified in code. (1) Commit b097efb changed App.tsx handleSpeak (lines 239-251) so that when ws.isConnected, ONLY ws.speak(text) fires; the browser speakText path â€” the sole code in src/ that ever sets u.lang='hi-IN' (App.tsx:150-153, including Devanagari auto-detect under ttsLanguage='auto') â€” is now unreachable in the normal connected state. (2) useWebSocket.tsx:608 confirms the protocol carries no language/rate/volume: send('speak', { text }). (3) python/main.py:1860 routes to _speak (2560-2566) -> TTSEngine.speak -> pyttsx3 engine.say() with a single voice pinned at init (_init_engine, 686-705); grep confirms zero language handling anywhere in the backend TTS path. (4) Empirically reproduced on this machine: SAPI5 (the engine pyttsx3 wraps) synthesizing the Hindi emergency phrase produced a 46-byte header-only WAV vs 96,150 bytes for the English phrase; only Microsoft David/Zira (en-US) SAPI voices are installed. (5) GlobalNavBar.tsx:195-201 confirms the showHindi emergency phrase appends Devanagari text, routed to handleSpeak (App.tsx:283). So with the backend connected â€” the default full-app state, including --simulate â€” the Hindi half of the emergency phrase and any pure-Hindi phrase produce zero audio, and no installed voice pack can fix it because the hi-IN metadata never reaches any engine. CLAUDE.md lists bilingual English+Hindi as a core requirement. Caveat on the counterfactual: on this exact dev machine (no Hindi voice), the old browser hi-IN path would also have fallen back to an English voice and produced silence, so the audible outcome here is unchanged; the regression fully manifests on a rig with the Windows Hindi voice pack (Hemant/Kalpana), where the old code spoke Hindi and the new code guarantees silence. The structural break â€” Hindi TTS impossible while connected â€” is unconditional and introduced by this diff. The double-speak the commit fixes was real for English, but the fix forecloses Hindi entirely.
suggestedFix: In App.tsx handleSpeak, keep the backend as the primary voice for English but route Hindi-bearing utterances through the language-aware path: if ttsLanguage==='hindi' or the text contains Devanagari (/[à¤€-à¥¿]/), call speakText(text, ttsRate, ttsVolume, ttsLanguage||'auto') even when connected (and skip ws.speak for that utterance to avoid reintroducing double-speak); else use ws.speak. Better long-term: extend the protocol to send('speak', { text, lang }) and have TTSEngine select a hi-IN SAPI voice per-utterance when one is installed (engine.setProperty('voice', hiVoiceId) before say()), reporting capability back to the frontend so it can fall back to browser synthesis when the backend has no Hindi voice. For the mixed emergency phrase, split the string at the script boundary and speak each segment with its matching engine/voice so the English half is never delayed by the Hindi half.
